## 8ffb572d-40ce-4af5-bb11-6805e629fde0
The official Figma MCP server. Use this server whenever the user wants to create, generate, edit, implement, or sync any design, UI, screen, component, mockup, or visual — in Figma, FigJam, Figma Make, or Figma Slides — and whenever the user mentions Figma or provides a figma.com URL.

This server bridges code and design in both directions, and supports designing from scratch using existing design systems and codebases.

CAPABILITIES:
- Read designs FROM Figma into code (get_design_context, get_screenshot, get_metadata, get_figjam)
- Write designs INTO Figma from code, intent, or existing components (use_figma, generate_figma_design, create_new_file, upload_assets)
- Bridge code and design via Code Connect (get_code_connect_map, add_code_connect_map, list_code_components, get_code_component_info)
- Create diagrams and FigJam content (generate_diagram, get_figjam)

WHEN TO USE THESE TOOLS:
- The user wants to create, mock up, or generate any UI, screen, component, or design — even if Figma isn't named
- The user wants to implement a design as code (design-to-code)
- The user wants to push a page, view, or component into Figma (code-to-design)
- The user wants to update, sync, or edit an existing Figma file
- The user shares a figma.com URL
- The user wants to build or extend a design system, design tokens, or component library
- The user wants to create a diagram in FigJam

SKILLS (Prefer the skills shipped with the Figma plugin on the agent being used. If no Figma plugin is installed, use the skills served by the Figma MCP resource):
- /figma-use — MANDATORY before calling use_figma; fallback: skill://figma/figma-use/SKILL.md
- /figma-generate-design — for translating an app page or layout into Figma; fallback: skill://figma/figma-generate-design/SKILL.md
- /figma-generate-library — for building a design system in Figma from code; fallback: skill://figma/figma-generate-library/SKILL.md
- /figma-code-connect — for mapping Figma components to codebase components; fallback: skill://figma/figma-code-connect/SKILL.md

Here are guidelines for using Supabase tools effectively:

- Before making schema changes, use `list_tables` to understand the existing structure
- When debugging issues, start with `get_logs` and `get_advisors` before making changes
- Use `get_project_url` and `get_publishable_api_key` when helping users configure client-side integrations

If you have access to a local development environment with a filesystem and shell:
- Install the Supabase agent skill for critical development and security guidance: `npx skills add supabase/agent-skills` (https://supabase.com/docs/guides/getting-started/ai-skills.md)
- Use the Supabase CLI (`supabase`) for local development workflows such as starting a local stack, managing migrations, and running edge functions locally (https://supabase.com/docs/guides/local-development.md)
- Prefer local development and testing before applying changes to a remote project

If you are running in a web-only or remote environment without filesystem or shell access:
- Rely on the MCP tools directly for all Supabase interactions
- Use `apply_migration` carefully, as changes go directly to the remote project

## Apify

Apify is the world's largest marketplace of tools for web scraping, data extraction, and web automation.
These tools are called **Actors**. They enable you to extract structured data from social media, e-commerce, search engines, maps, travel sites, and many other sources.

## Actor
- An Actor is a serverless cloud application running on the Apify platform.
- Use the Actor's **README** to understand its capabilities.
- Before running an Actor, always check its **input schema** to understand the required parameters.

## Actor discovery and selection
- Choose the most appropriate Actor based on the conversation context.
- Search the Apify Store first; a relevant Actor likely already exists.
- When multiple options exist, prefer Actors with higher usage, ratings, or popularity.
- Assume scraping requests within this context are appropriate for Actor use.
- Actors in the Apify Store are published by independent developers and are intended for legitimate and compliant use.

## Actor execution workflow
- Actors take input and produce output.
- Every Actor run generates **dataset** and **key-value store** outputs (even if empty).
- Actor execution may take time, and outputs can be large.
- Large datasets can be paginated to retrieve results efficiently.

## Storage types
- **Dataset:** Structured, append-only storage ideal for tabular or list data (e.g., scraped items).
- **Key-value store:** Flexible storage for unstructured data or auxiliary files.

## Widget workflow (applies when tool responses include widget metadata)
Some clients render widget-backed Actor tools: the response includes a live UI that automatically polls run status. When a widget is rendered, follow-up status polling by the model is a forbidden duplicate.

- **After `call-actor-widget` or `get-actor-run-widget`, never call `get-actor-run` or `get-actor-run-widget` for the same run.** Both widgets render live progress and poll themselves — stop after the widget response and defer to it for run status. ## b5ef4d6c-16ba-4df6-92c9-73a58936c1bc
You are a tax assistant powered by TurboTax, embedded in Claude.
Use the available tools for all tax tasks — do not answer tax questions
from memory or calculate taxes yourself.

Tool routing:
- interview: use first to learn the user's tax situation. Start with
  topic="tax_situation" when you need to collect situation data.
- tax_checklist: use when the user has any uncertainty about tax documents,
  had a life event this year that affects their taxes, asks for general
  help getting started with taxes, or seems overwhelmed and unsure where to begin.
  Call interview(topic="tax_situation") first if no situation data exists.
- document_upload: use when the user wants to upload a tax form or document.
- tax_estimate: use when the user explicitly wants a refund or owe estimate
  and has provided enough financial details.
- tax_expert_connect: use when the user wants to speak with or book a
  tax expert.

Some tools require IRC §7216 consent. If a tool requests consent, show the
consent widget and wait for the user to accept or decline — never proceed
on their behalf.

## computer-use
You have a computer-use MCP available (tools named `mcp__computer-use__*`). It lets you take screenshots of the user's desktop and control it with mouse clicks, keyboard input, and scrolling.

**Pick the right tool for the app.** Each tier trades speed/precision against coverage:

1. **Dedicated MCP for the app** — if the task is in an app that has its own MCP (Slack, Gmail, Calendar, Linear, etc.) and that MCP is connected, use it. API-backed tools are fast and precise.
2. **Chrome MCP** (`mcp__claude-in-chrome__*`) — if the target is a web app and there's no dedicated MCP for it, use the browser tools. DOM-aware, much faster than clicking pixels. If the Chrome extension isn't connected, ask the user to install it rather than falling through to computer use.
3. **Computer use** — for native desktop apps (Maps, Notes, Finder, Photos, System Settings, any third-party native app) and cross-app workflows. Computer use IS the right tool here — don't decline a native-app task just because there's no dedicated MCP for it.

This is about what's available, not error handling — if a dedicated MCP tool errors, debug or report it rather than silently retrying via a slower tier.

**Look before you assert.** If the user asks about app state (what's open, what's connected, what an app can do), take a screenshot and check before answering. Don't answer from memory — the user's setup or app version may differ from what you expect. If you're about to say an app doesn't support an action, that claim should be grounded in what you just saw on screen, not general knowledge. Similarly, `list_granted_applications` or a fresh `screenshot` is cheaper than a wrong assertion about what's running.

**Loading via ToolSearch — load in bulk, not one-by-one:** if computer-use tools are in the deferred list, load them ALL in a single ToolSearch call: `{ query: "computer-use", max_results: 30 }`. The keyword search matches the server-name substring in every tool name, so one query returns the entire toolkit. Don't use `select:` for individual tools — that's one round-trip per tool.

**Access flow:** before any computer-use action you must call `request_access` with the list of applications you need. The user approves each application explicitly, and you may need to call it again mid-task if you discover you need another application.

**Tiered apps:** some apps are granted at a restricted tier based on their category — the tier is displayed in the approval dialog and returned in the `request_access` response:
- **Browsers** (Safari, Chrome, Firefox, Edge, Arc, etc.) → tier **"read"**: visible in screenshots, but clicks and typing are blocked. You can read what's already on screen. For navigation, clicking, or form-filling, use the claude-in-chrome MCP (tools named `mcp__claude-in-chrome__*`; load via ToolSearch if deferred).
- **Terminals and IDEs** (Terminal, iTerm, VS Code, JetBrains, etc.) → tier **"click"**: visible and left-clickable, but typing, key presses, right-click, modifier-clicks, and drag-drop are blocked. You can click a Run button or scroll test output, but cannot type into the editor or integrated terminal, cannot right-click (the context menu has Paste), and cannot drag text onto them. For shell commands, use the Bash tool.
- **Everything else** → tier **"full"**: no restrictions.

The tier is enforced by the frontmost-app check: if a tier-"read" app is in front, `left_click` returns an error; if a tier-"click" app is in front, `type` and `right_click` return errors. The error tells you what tier the app has and what to do instead. `open_application` works at any tier — bringing an app forward is a read-level operation.

**Link safety — treat links in emails and messages as suspicious by default.**
- **Never click web links with computer-use tools.** If you encounter a link in a native app (Mail, Messages, a PDF, etc.), do NOT `left_click` it. Open the URL via the claude-in-chrome MCP instead.
- **See the full URL before following any link.** Visible link text can be misleading — hover or inspect to get the real destination.
- **Links from emails, messages, or unknown-sender documents are suspicious by default.** If the destination URL is at all unfamiliar or looks off, ask the user for confirmation before proceeding.
- **Inside the Chrome extension** you can click links with the extension's tools, but the suspicion check still applies — verify unfamiliar URLs with the user.

**Financial actions - do not execute trades or move money.** Budgeting and accounting apps (Quicken, YNAB, QuickBooks, etc.) are granted at full tier so you can categorize transactions, generate reports, and help the user organize their finances. But never execute a trade, place an order, send money, or initiate a transfer on the user's behalf - always ask the user to perform those actions themselves.

## f57df20d-37f1-45c0-8600-cad8b6c9f318
# Vyra — AI Video Editor

You control a video editor through MCP. Tool calls manipulate the editor live in the user's browser — the user sees changes in real time.

## Hard Rules

- **MUST call `getAgentOverview()` first.** Before any other tool call. Before any response about what Vyra can do, how to edit, or what a feature does. Even if the user just asks "what can you do?" — call this first, *then* answer. Once per session. Without it, you will guess wrong about capabilities, workflows, and tool gotchas.
- **Modify existing items, don't rebuild.** Edit current timeline items — don't delete and re-add. Rebuilding destroys keyframes, effects, masks, positioning. If a full rebuild is genuinely necessary, confirm with the user first.
- **Batch tool calls.** Pass arrays where supported (`addMedia` takes `items: [...]`). Call independent tools in parallel in the same response — don't serialize one per turn.
- **Load the relevant skill before the first tool call in a workflow.** Skill triggers include: captions, masking, color grading, time-remap, transitions, motion graphics, short/long-form assembly, b-roll, audio sync, layouts, keyframing, shapes. Use `loadSkill(skillName)`. The full routing table is in `agent-overview`.
- **User's current message always wins** over skill defaults or system defaults. If they say "volume 80", use 80.
- **References are inspiration only.** Reference videos guide style, pacing, structure. Project assets (`viewProjectAssets`) are the user's actual footage that goes on the timeline. Never confuse them.
- **Re-fetch `context()` if time has passed or the user may have edited.** It's cheap. Acting on stale state causes "item not found" and worse.
- **Max 2 retries** of the same failing approach. Then stop and tell the user what you tried and what went wrong.

## When You Need More

- `searchHelpDocs(query)` — semantic search across user-facing help docs
- Tool descriptions cover per-tool params and gotchas

## pencil
The Pencil MCP server is the editor for .pen design files (web/mobile apps and websites). Use it to read, generate, and validate designs.

.pen files are encrypted: access them only via "pencil" MCP tools — never use Read or Grep on .pen files. Follow each tool's input schema exactly.

Tools: get_editor_state, open_document, get_guidelines, batch_get, batch_design, snapshot_layout, get_screenshot, get_variables, set_variables, find_empty_space_on_canvas, search_all_unique_properties, replace_all_matching_properties, export_nodes.

1	// app/src/db/schema.ts — Drizzle table handles for typed queries.
2	//
3	// `schema.sql` (generated from docs/erd-physical.md) is the CANONICAL DDL — it creates the
4	// tables, CHECK constraints, composite FKs, partial indexes, and triggers that SQLite/Drizzle
5	// can't all express in TS. These Drizzle definitions MIRROR that DDL purely so repositories can
6	// build type-safe queries; they intentionally omit the CHECKs/triggers (the DB enforces those).
7	// snake_case DB columns ↔ camelCase TS properties (see the ERD's Drizzle/zod mapping table).
8	
9	import { sql } from "drizzle-orm";
10	import { integer, real, sqliteTable, text } from "drizzle-orm/sqlite-core";
11	import type { ClaimStatus } from "../domain/entities/claim";
12	import type { LineItemStatus } from "../domain/entities/line-item";
13	import type { ReasonCode } from "../domain/reason-codes";
14	
15	// All timestamps default to ISO-8601 UTC at the DB layer (metadata; never read by logic).
16	const nowDefault = sql`(strftime('%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%fZ','now'))`;
17	
18	export const members = sqliteTable("members", {
19	  id: text("id").primaryKey(),
20	  name: text("name").notNull(), // PHI
21	  dob: text("dob").notNull(), // PHI
22	  createdAt: text("created_at").notNull().default(nowDefault),
23	});
24	
25	export const policies = sqliteTable("policies", {
26	  id: text("id").primaryKey(),
27	  memberId: text("member_id")
28	    .notNull()
29	    .references(() => members.id),
30	  planYear: text("plan_year").notNull(),
31	  effectiveDate: text("effective_date").notNull(),
32	  terminationDate: text("termination_date").notNull(),
33	  deductibleCents: integer("deductible_cents").notNull(),
34	  oopMaxCents: integer("oop_max_cents").notNull(),
35	  createdAt: text("created_at").notNull().default(nowDefault),
36	});
37	
38	export const coverageRules = sqliteTable("coverage_rules", {
39	  id: text("id").primaryKey(),
40	  policyId: text("policy_id")
41	    .notNull()
42	    .references(() => policies.id),
43	  serviceCode: text("service_code").notNull(),
44	  covered: integer("covered", { mode: "boolean" }).notNull(),
45	  excluded: integer("excluded", { mode: "boolean" }).notNull(),
46	  costShareType: text("cost_share_type", {
47	    enum: ["full_coverage", "copay", "coinsurance"],
48	  }).notNull(),
49	  copayCents: integer("copay_cents"), // non-null iff type=copay (CHECK in schema.sql)
50	  coinsuranceRate: real("coinsurance_rate"), // non-null iff type=coinsurance
51	  appliesDeductible: integer("applies_deductible", {
52	    mode: "boolean",
53	  }).notNull(),
54	  limitUnit: text("limit_unit", {
55	    enum: ["none", "dollars", "visits"],
56	  }).notNull(),
57	  limitAmountCents: integer("limit_amount_cents"), // non-null iff unit=dollars
58	  limitCount: integer("limit_count"), // non-null iff unit=visits
59	  requiresPriorAuth: integer("requires_prior_auth", {
60	    mode: "boolean",
61	  }).notNull(),
62	  createdAt: text("created_at").notNull().default(nowDefault),
63	});
64	
65	export const claims = sqliteTable("claims", {
66	  id: text("id").primaryKey(),
67	  memberId: text("member_id")
68	    .notNull()
69	    .references(() => members.id),
70	  policyId: text("policy_id")
71	    .notNull()
72	    .references(() => policies.id),
73	  serviceDate: text("service_date").notNull(),
74	  provider: text("provider"), // PHI, nullable
75	  diagnosisCode: text("diagnosis_code"), // PHI, nullable
76	  status: text("status").$type<ClaimStatus>().notNull().default("SUBMITTED"),
77	  claimSeq: integer("claim_seq").notNull().default(0),
78	  createdAt: text("created_at").notNull().default(nowDefault),
79	  updatedAt: text("updated_at").notNull().default(nowDefault),
80	});
81	
82	export const lineItems = sqliteTable("line_items", {
83	  id: text("id").primaryKey(),
84	  claimId: text("claim_id")
85	    .notNull()
86	    .references(() => claims.id),
87	  serviceCode: text("service_code").notNull(), // free text (unlisted → NO_COVERAGE at adj)
88	  billedCents: integer("billed_cents").notNull(),
89	  units: integer("units").notNull().default(1),
90	  priorAuthPresent: integer("prior_auth_present", { mode: "boolean" })
91	    .notNull()
92	    .default(true),
93	  status: text("status").$type<LineItemStatus>().notNull().default("PENDING"),
94	  fingerprint: text("fingerprint").notNull(),
95	  createdAt: text("created_at").notNull().default(nowDefault),
96	  updatedAt: text("updated_at").notNull().default(nowDefault),
97	});
98	
99	export const adjudications = sqliteTable("adjudications", {
100	  id: text("id").primaryKey(),
101	  lineItemId: text("line_item_id")
102	    .notNull()
103	    .references(() => lineItems.id),
104	  planYear: text("plan_year").notNull(),
105	  seq: integer("seq").notNull(), // per-line clock; current = MAX(seq)
106	  status: text("status").$type<"APPROVED" | "DENIED">().notNull(),
107	  billedCents: integer("billed_cents").notNull(), // snapshot at decision time
108	  payableCents: integer("payable_cents").notNull(),
109	  memberResponsibilityCents: integer("member_responsibility_cents").notNull(),
110	  reasonsJson: text("reasons_json", { mode: "json" })
111	    .$type<ReasonCode[]>()
112	    .notNull(),
113	  explanation: text("explanation").notNull(),
114	  deltaDeductibleIncCents: integer("delta_deductible_inc_cents")
115	    .notNull()
116	    .default(0),
117	  deltaOopIncCents: integer("delta_oop_inc_cents").notNull().default(0),
118	  deltaLimitInc: integer("delta_limit_inc").notNull().default(0),
119	  createdAt: text("created_at").notNull().default(nowDefault),
120	});
121	
122	export const accumulators = sqliteTable("accumulators", {
123	  id: text("id").primaryKey(),
124	  memberId: text("member_id")
125	    .notNull()
126	    .references(() => members.id),
127	  planYear: text("plan_year").notNull(),
128	  dimension: text("dimension").notNull(), // DEDUCTIBLE | OOP | LIMIT:<service_code>
129	  unit: text("unit", { enum: ["CENTS", "COUNT"] }).notNull(),
130	  usedCents: integer("used_cents").notNull().default(0),
131	  usedCount: integer("used_count").notNull().default(0),
132	  updatedAt: text("updated_at").notNull().default(nowDefault),
133	});
134	
135	export const disputes = sqliteTable("disputes", {
136	  id: text("id").primaryKey(),
137	  lineItemId: text("line_item_id")
138	    .notNull()
139	    .references(() => lineItems.id),
140	  // original/resolved adjudication ids use COMPOSITE FKs (id,line_item_id) — enforced in schema.sql.
141	  originalAdjudicationId: text("original_adjudication_id").notNull(),
142	  resolvedAdjudicationId: text("resolved_adjudication_id"), // null while OPEN
143	  reason: text("reason").notNull(),
144	  correctedPriorAuthPresent: integer("corrected_prior_auth_present", {
145	    mode: "boolean",
146	  }),
147	  correctedServiceCode: text("corrected_service_code"),
148	  correctedBilledCents: integer("corrected_billed_cents"),
149	  correctedUnits: integer("corrected_units"),
150	  outcome: text("outcome", {
151	    enum: ["UPHELD", "OVERTURNED", "PARTIALLY_OVERTURNED", "MODIFIED"],
152	  }), // null while OPEN
153	  state: text("state", { enum: ["OPEN", "RESOLVED"] })
154	    .notNull()
155	    .default("OPEN"),
156	  openedAt: text("opened_at").notNull().default(nowDefault),
157	  resolvedAt: text("resolved_at"), // null while OPEN
158	});
159	
160	export const statusTransitions = sqliteTable("status_transitions", {
161	  id: text("id").primaryKey(),
162	  entityType: text("entity_type", { enum: ["CLAIM", "LINE_ITEM"] }).notNull(),
163	  claimId: text("claim_id")
164	    .notNull()
165	    .references(() => claims.id), // owning claim aggregate; always set
166	  lineItemId: text("line_item_id").references(() => lineItems.id), // set iff LINE_ITEM
167	  fromStatus: text("from_status"), // null on create
168	  toStatus: text("to_status").notNull(),
169	  actor: text("actor", { enum: ["SYSTEM", "MEMBER"] }).notNull(),
170	  reason: text("reason", {
171	    enum: ["SUBMIT", "ADJUDICATED", "AGGREGATED", "DISPUTE_REOPEN"],
172	  }).notNull(),
173	  seq: integer("seq").notNull(), // claim-aggregate logical clock
174	  createdAt: text("created_at").notNull().default(nowDefault),
175	});

content.content:
1	# Self-review
2	
3	A calibrated gap-list. The reviewer cross-checks this against the code and the tests; if it
4	claims more than the code delivers, that costs more than an honest gap would.
5	
6	## What's good
7	
8	**Deterministic, explainable adjudication.** The adjudicator is a pure function:
9	`(line, policy, rule, accumulator snapshot) → decision + deltas`. No I/O, no side effects.
10	21 unit tests cover every gate (NO_COVERAGE, EXCLUDED, POLICY_NOT_ACTIVE, PRIOR_AUTH_REQUIRED,
11	LIMIT_EXCEEDED, DUPLICATE_LINE_ITEM) and every cost-share path (full_coverage, copay, coinsurance
12	with deductible draw, dollar-limit straddle, OOP cap). The cross-line determinism test confirms
13	that line 2 sees line 1's deductible draw and that re-running against the same snapshot produces
14	identical output — verifiable in `app/tests/adjudicate-line.test.ts`.
15	
16	**Integer cents with a provably-satisfied sum invariant.** All money is integer cents. The
17	coinsurance rounding test explicitly asserts `member + plan === allowed` on an odd-cent remainder —
18	the one place where float math would silently lose a cent. The invariant holds for every cost-share
19	path because `plan = allowed − member` is always computed last, never independently.
20	
21	**Coverage rules as typed config data.** Adding a new service to the system is a single
22	`INSERT INTO coverage_rules` — zero code changes. The adjudicator is an ordered switch over the
23	rule's `costShare.type`; the rule record carries all the parameters. This is visible in both the
24	seed matrix (10 members × up to 3 rules each, zero adjudicator changes) and the test helpers
25	(rules passed as plain objects to `seedWorld()`).
26	
27	**Append-only adjudication history.** Adjudications never overwrite. A dispute appends a new row at
28	a higher `seq` (tested in cycles 27 + 32–36); the original decision is preserved immutably and
29	surfaced on `GET /claims/:id` as `adjudication_history`. The status-transition log (`status_transitions`
30	table) is also append-only with a single `setStatus()` chokepoint — the log can never drift from the
31	status columns because both writes happen in the same DB statement.
32	
33	**TDD trail visible in git history.** Tests appear before or alongside implementation across 36
34	numbered cycles. The commit messages follow a `test:` → `feat:` discipline, and the
35	`app/tests/*.test.ts` files assert domain behavior (amounts, statuses, reason codes, accumulator
36	deltas) — not just HTTP status codes. Reading the test files top-to-bottom is a readable spec of the
37	domain rules.
38	
39	## What's rough
40	
41	**Duplicate detection is hardcoded `false` at the service layer.** The claim fingerprint
42	(`memberId + serviceCode + serviceDate + billedCents`) is computed at intake and stored on every line
43	item. The pure adjudicator accepts an `alreadyAdjudicated: boolean` flag and correctly emits
44	`DUPLICATE_LINE_ITEM` when it is `true`. But `claimService.adjudicateClaim` hardcodes that flag to
45	`false` — the cross-claim repo query (`findByFingerprint`) was deferred, so submitting an identical
46	line a second time does not currently deny it. The structure is in place; the query is a 10-line gap.
47	
48	**Explanation strings are inlined as template literals.** Each adjudication decision carries a plain
49	English `explanation` sentence generated inline in the adjudicator. This works and is tested
50	(the `GET /claims/:id` response asserts `explanation` is non-empty and matches a pattern), but
51	the strings are not templated from a lookup table. Real EOB systems use CARC/RARC code mappings with member-appropriate phrasing. The
52	current approach is serviceable for a demo; it would need a template layer before it could be
53	reviewed or localized by a non-engineer.
54	
55	**Dispute scope is single-line; sibling totals are not recalculated.** When a dispute overturns a
56	line, the disputed line's payable changes. The claim-level `totalPayableCents` on the response is
57	recomputed correctly (it aggregates all current decisions). But the accumulator for sibling lines
58	that were adjudicated before this dispute is not retroactively corrected — documented as a design
59	decision (decision #16), not a bug, but a real limitation for any scenario where the overturn changes
60	the accumulator state siblings depend on.
61	
62	## What's thin or skipped
63	
64	| Skipped / thin | Why | What it would take |
65	|---|---|---|
66	| Duplicate detection (cross-claim) | Fingerprint is stored; the repo query was deferred | `findByFingerprint()` call in `claimService` before adjudication |
67	| Out-of-network coverage | In-network only; allowed == billed | A parallel OON rule set + network flag on the line item |
68	| Fee schedule (allowed ≠ billed) | Allowed = billed keeps the math clear in the demo | A provider/fee-schedule lookup step before the cost-share switch |
69	| Concurrency / multi-writer | SQLite single-writer is fine for a demo | Row-level locking or Postgres + transaction isolation |
70	| Prior-auth as a sub-workflow | Modeled as a boolean precondition | A pending-auth sub-state machine with an approval queue |
71	| `PAID` state / settle action | The payable amount is computed; settlement needs a payment gateway | An explicit settle endpoint + `PAID` transition + gateway stub |
72	| Family / aggregate deductible | Per-member accumulator only | A family-plan link + a shared accumulator ledger |

file.content:
# Self-review

A calibrated gap-list. The reviewer cross-checks this against the code and the tests; if it
claims more than the code delivers, that costs more than an honest gap would.

## What's good

**Deterministic, explainable adjudication.** The adjudicator is a pure function:
`(line, policy, rule, accumulator snapshot) → decision + deltas`. No I/O, no side effects.
21 unit tests cover every gate (NO_COVERAGE, EXCLUDED, POLICY_NOT_ACTIVE, PRIOR_AUTH_REQUIRED,
LIMIT_EXCEEDED, DUPLICATE_LINE_ITEM) and every cost-share path (full_coverage, copay, coinsurance
with deductible draw, dollar-limit straddle, OOP cap). The cross-line determinism test confirms
that line 2 sees line 1's deductible draw and that re-running against the same snapshot produces
identical output — verifiable in `app/tests/adjudicate-line.test.ts`.

**Integer cents with a provably-satisfied sum invariant.** All money is integer cents. The
coinsurance rounding test explicitly asserts `member + plan === allowed` on an odd-cent remainder —
the one place where float math would silently lose a cent. The invariant holds for every cost-share
path because `plan = allowed − member` is always computed last, never independently.

**Coverage rules as typed config data.** Adding a new service to the system is a single
`INSERT INTO coverage_rules` — zero code changes. The adjudicator is an ordered switch over the
rule's `costShare.type`; the rule record carries all the parameters. This is visible in both the
seed matrix (10 members × up to 3 rules each, zero adjudicator changes) and the test helpers
(rules passed as plain objects to `seedWorld()`).

**Append-only adjudication history.** Adjudications never overwrite. A dispute appends a new row at
a higher `seq` (tested in cycles 27 + 32–36); the original decision is preserved immutably and
surfaced on `GET /claims/:id` as `adjudication_history`. The status-transition log (`status_transitions`
table) is also append-only with a single `setStatus()` chokepoint — the log can never drift from the
status columns because both writes happen in the same DB statement.

**TDD trail visible in git history.** Tests appear before or alongside implementation across 36
numbered cycles. The commit messages follow a `test:` → `feat:` discipline, and the
`app/tests/*.test.ts` files assert domain behavior (amounts, statuses, reason codes, accumulator
deltas) — not just HTTP status codes. Reading the test files top-to-bottom is a readable spec of the
domain rules.

## What's rough

**Duplicate detection is hardcoded `false` at the service layer.** The claim fingerprint
(`memberId + serviceCode + serviceDate + billedCents`) is computed at intake and stored on every line
item. The pure adjudicator accepts an `alreadyAdjudicated: boolean` flag and correctly emits
`DUPLICATE_LINE_ITEM` when it is `true`. But `claimService.adjudicateClaim` hardcodes that flag to
`false` — the cross-claim repo query (`findByFingerprint`) was deferred, so submitting an identical
line a second time does not currently deny it. The structure is in place; the query is a 10-line gap.

**Explanation strings are inlined as template literals.** Each adjudication decision carries a plain
English `explanation` sentence generated inline in the adjudicator. This works and is tested
(the `GET /claims/:id` response asserts `explanation` is non-empty and matches a pattern), but
the strings are not templated from a lookup table. Real EOB systems use CARC/RARC code mappings with member-appropriate phrasing. The
current approach is serviceable for a demo; it would need a template layer before it could be
reviewed or localized by a non-engineer.

**Dispute scope is single-line; sibling totals are not recalculated.** When a dispute overturns a
line, the disputed line's payable changes. The claim-level `totalPayableCents` on the response is
recomputed correctly (it aggregates all current decisions). But the accumulator for sibling lines
that were adjudicated before this dispute is not retroactively corrected — documented as a design
decision (decision #16), not a bug, but a real limitation for any scenario where the overturn changes
the accumulator state siblings depend on.

## What's thin or skipped

| Skipped / thin | Why | What it would take |
|---|---|---|
| Duplicate detection (cross-claim) | Fingerprint is stored; the repo query was deferred | `findByFingerprint()` call in `claimService` before adjudication |
| Out-of-network coverage | In-network only; allowed == billed | A parallel OON rule set + network flag on the line item |
| Fee schedule (allowed ≠ billed) | Allowed = billed keeps the math clear in the demo | A provider/fee-schedule lookup step before the cost-share switch |
| Concurrency / multi-writer | SQLite single-writer is fine for a demo | Row-level locking or Postgres + transaction isolation |
| Prior-auth as a sub-workflow | Modeled as a boolean precondition | A pending-auth sub-state machine with an approval queue |
| `PAID` state / settle action | The payable amount is computed; settlement needs a payment gateway | An explicit settle endpoint + `PAID` transition + gateway stub |
| Family / aggregate deductible | Per-member accumulator only | A family-plan link + a shared accumulator ledger |
| CARC/RARC code mapping | Explanation text is inline; real EOBs carry specific codes | A reason-code → CARC/RARC lookup table |

## Confidence calibration

| Area | Confidence | Note |
|---|---|---|
| Adjudication order and gate logic | **High** | 21 unit tests; each gate has its own describe block with an isolation guarantee |
| Money rounding (coinsurance odd cents) | **High** | Explicit test asserts `member + plan === allowed` on an odd-cent remainder |
| State-machine completeness | **High** | Transition log tests (cycles 28–31) cover every status write; `setStatus()` is the only write path |
| Explanation text accuracy | **Medium** | Strings are asserted non-empty and pattern-matched; not independently verified against a template spec |
| Accumulator carry-forward (within a claim) | **High** | Cross-line determinism test (cycles 21–22) confirms line 2 sees line 1's deltas |
| Accumulator carry-forward (cross-claim) | **Medium** | The upsert logic is tested via a single writeback test; no integration test submits two claims sequentially and checks the carry |
| Duplicate line detection (cross-claim) | **Low** | Hardcoded `false` in the service; the pure-function path is tested, the DB-backed path is not |

Legend: **High** = covered by tests I trust; **Medium** = works, lightly tested; **Low** = wired but
under-tested or gapped, flagged.

## What I'd do with more time

1. **Close the duplicate-detection gap.** Add a `findByFingerprint()` repo method and call it in
   `claimService.adjudicateClaim` before adjudication — the path through `adjudicateLine` is already
   implemented and tested; only the DB query is missing.
2. **Property-based tests for the money invariant.** Generate random `billedCents`, `rate`, and
   accumulator snapshots and assert `member + plan === allowed` for all coinsurance combinations and
   OOP-cap crossings.
3. **Sequential cross-claim accumulator test.** Submit claim A, read the accumulators, submit claim B,
   confirm deductible and OOP carry forward correctly.
4. **Out-of-network rule set.** A parallel rule set with a network flag, so the adjudicator can say
   "covered at 50% OON" rather than "NO_COVERAGE".
5. **Fee schedule.** A negotiated-rate lookup so `allowed ≠ billed`, with the adjustment explained in
   the EOB sentence.
6. **CARC/RARC code mapping.** Replace inline explanation strings with a templated lookup table
   reviewable by non-engineers.
7. **Reviewer queue for disputes.** The `DISPUTED_OVERRIDE` enum slot is reserved; a reviewer-approval
   path would layer on the existing dispute state machine without touching the adjudicator.

## Honest one-paragraph summary

The strongest part of the system is the adjudication engine: a pure function, 21 unit tests, provably
deterministic, and covering every gate and cost-share path including the coinsurance rounding edge case.
The integer-cents invariant (`member + plan == allowed`) is tested explicitly, and the append-only audit
trail (adjudications + status-transitions) makes the lifecycle traceable end-to-end. The single biggest
weakness is the missing cross-claim duplicate check: the fingerprint is computed and stored on every line
item, the pure-adjudicator path for `DUPLICATE_LINE_ITEM` is tested, but the service-layer query was
deferred and is hardcoded `false` — a second identical claim currently adjudicates normally instead of
being denied. That is the first thing I would fix.

content.text:
Both findings handled. Here's the breakdown.

## ✅ Finding #1 — `alreadyAdjudicated` hardcoded `false` (the real bug)

**Root cause:** the pure adjudicator already denies duplicates when told `alreadyAdjudicated: true`, but `claim.service.ts` never asked the DB — it passed a literal `false`. So a resubmitted line was silently re-approved.

**Fix (test-first):**

| File | Change |
|---|---|
| `app/tests/duplicate-line.test.ts` 🆕 | RED test: 3 cases (cross-claim dup, within-claim dup, distinct-lines-not-flagged) |
| [adjudication.repository.ts](app/src/repositories/adjudication.repository.ts:76) | New `existsForFingerprint(fingerprint, excludeLineItemId)` — `COUNT(*)` over `adjudications ⋈ line_items` |
| [claim.service.ts:145](app/src/services/claim.service.ts:145) | `alreadyAdjudicated: false` → `deps.adjudications.existsForFingerprint(fingerprint, line.id)` |

**Why the `adjudications` JOIN (not just `line_items`):** within one claim, all sibling lines are inserted *before* Phase-2 adjudication runs. Joining on `adjudications` means only lines **already decided** count as a prior — so the first occurrence passes, the duplicate is denied. Excluding `line.id` makes it order-independent. 🔑

> Example: submit `PREVENTIVE / $120` twice → 1st claim `APPROVED`, 2nd claim `DENIED [DUPLICATE_LINE_ITEM]`.

**Verification:** `65 passed` (62 + 3 new) · `tsc --noEmit` clean · biome clean on touched files.

## ⚠️ Finding #2 — 6 test commits vs 18 feat commits

This is an **observation about *past* git history**, not a present code defect — some HTTP-layer commits landed `feat:` before `test:`. It's **not retroactively "fixable"**: `main` is already pushed to `origin`, and rewriting published history (`rebase`/`filter`) is destructive and would break anyone who pulled it. ❌

What *is* actionable — and what I did — is land **this** change with clean discipline: a verified RED → GREEN cycle that becomes one `test:` commit + one `feat:` commit, adding to the clean-TDD column.

I did **not** commit (your standing rule: commit only when asked). When you're ready, the clean pair would be:

```
test: add duplicate-line detection cases (cycle 7)   # app/tests/duplicate-line.test.ts
feat: deny resubmitted lines via existsForFingerprint # repo + service
```

*Jargon:* **fingerprint** = `memberId|serviceCode|serviceDate|billedCents`, the identity used to spot a resubmitted line. **RED→GREEN** = TDD's write-failing-test-then-make-it-pass loop.

---

### 3 follow-up questions to move faster
1. Want me to **commit** these as the `test:` → `feat:` pair above (and update `docs/self-review.md`, which still lists duplicate detection as a "Low confidence / gapped" item)?
2. Should duplicate detection be **scoped to plan year** (it's currently global by fingerprint — `serviceDate` already pins the year, so cross-year collisions can't occur — confirm that's the intended boundary)?
3. The same `alreadyAdjudicated: false` is hardcoded in [dispute.service.ts:178](app/src/services/dispute.service.ts:178) — I left it (a dispute *re-adjudicates the same line*, which isn't a duplicate). Want me to confirm/document that as intentional?

content.text:
Here's the bug explained, end-to-end, with a concrete example.

## 🧩 What the line means

| Phrase | Plain meaning |
|---|---|
| `alreadyAdjudicated` | A yes/no flag: "has this exact line been decided before?" |
| **hardcoded `false`** | The code always passed `false` — i.e. "no, never seen before" — *without ever checking* |
| **pure adjudicator** | The decision function `adjudicateLine()` — no database, just logic in/out |
| **DB query missing** | Nobody asked the database "have we seen this line already?" |
| **fingerprint** | The line's identity: `memberId \| serviceCode \| serviceDate \| billedCents` |

## 🔎 The mechanics

The duplicate **rule existed** (in [adjudicator.ts:59](app/src/domain/adjudication/adjudicator.ts:59)):

```ts
if (alreadyAdjudicated) {
  return deny(ReasonCode.DUPLICATE_LINE_ITEM, ...); // ✅ logic was ready
}
```

But the **caller never set the flag** (old [claim.service.ts:145](app/src/services/claim.service.ts:145)):

```ts
adjudicateLine({ ...,
  alreadyAdjudicated: false,   // ❌ always false — the DB was never consulted
});
```

So the gate was a light switch wired to a button nobody pressed. 💡🚫

## 📖 Worked example — same claim submitted twice

A member sends **PREVENTIVE, $120** on 2026-06-19. Then the clinic's billing system **accidentally submits the identical claim again** a week later (a very common real-world event).

Fingerprint both times: `mem_seed_1|PREVENTIVE|2026-06-19|12000`

| Submission | What *should* happen | 🐞 Before the fix | ✅ After the fix |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1st** | Pay it | `APPROVED` → plan pays $120 | `APPROVED` → plan pays $120 |
| **2nd** (identical) | Catch the dup, pay nothing | `APPROVED` → **plan pays $120 AGAIN** 💸 | `DENIED [DUPLICATE_LINE_ITEM]` → plan pays $0 |

**Before:** the plan pays **$240 for one $120 service** — a double payment. At scale that's exactly how payers leak money.

**Why it happened:** on the 2nd submission the service still passed `alreadyAdjudicated: false`, so the duplicate gate was skipped and the claim flowed through the normal full-coverage path → approved again.

## 🛠️ The one-line gap that closed it

```ts
// after: actually ask the DB "was this fingerprint already decided?"
alreadyAdjudicated: deps.adjudications.existsForFingerprint(fingerprint, line.id),
```

`existsForFingerprint` runs `COUNT(*)` over `adjudications ⋈ line_items` where the fingerprint matches — so the 2nd submission finds the 1st one's decision and denies. (Verified: the new test submits the line twice and asserts `1st = APPROVED, 2nd = DENIED`.)

---

### 3 follow-up questions
1. Want me to add an **HTTP-level test** (`POST /claims` twice → 2nd response shows the line `DENIED`) so the fix is proven through the API, not just the service?
2. Should a duplicate also be **rejected at intake (4xx)** instead of being *adjudicated as DENIED* — i.e. is a duplicate a "bad request" or a "valid claim with a denied line"? (Current design = the latter.)
3. Real billing sometimes **legitimately repeats** a service (e.g. two identical lab draws same day). Do you want a way to mark a line as an intentional repeat so it *isn't* flagged?
Let me read the trackers to update them accurately.

# TRACK.md — live cross-session memory

The only file that mutates freely. Every agent updates it at session close. Keep it
honest and current — the next agent trusts it instead of re-deriving everything.

## Current focus

**Phase `04-coding` (TDD) — the entire pure core is done.** Cycles **1–25 complete and green**
(25 tests; working tree clean at `a11c081`):

- **1–21 — `adjudicateLine`** (pure): gates (no-coverage / excluded / not-covered /
  policy-not-active / prior-auth / duplicate), cost-share math (full / copay / coinsurance +
  deductible draw + half-up rounding), limits (visit cap + dollar straddle), OOP cap, and
  cross-line determinism (line 2 sees line 1's deltas; re-run is identical).
- **22–25 — `aggregateClaimStatus`** (pure): all-approved → `APPROVED`, all-denied → `DENIED`,
  mixed / straddle → `PARTIALLY_APPROVED`. Added the `ClaimStatus` type + a minimal
  `LineOutcome`; the `UNDER_REVIEW` branch is deliberately deferred to the dispute cycles (#24).

**ALL 36 cycles DONE (uncommitted) — the full TDD plan is complete.** Physical schema locked in
[`docs/erd-physical.md`](docs/erd-physical.md); the whole engine is implemented test-first on it:
- **Scaffolding (`chore:`):** deps (`better-sqlite3` + `drizzle-orm`), `app/src/db/schema.sql`
  (canonical DDL from the ERD), `createDb(':memory:')` factory + `applySchema`, Drizzle `schema.ts`
  (mirror), repo/service DI skeletons (**service → repositories → db**), schema smoke test.
- **26 writeback (`feat:`):** `claimService.adjudicateClaim` — resolve policy+rules → persist →
  adjudicate each line (reusing pure `adjudicateLine`) → append decisions → advance accumulators →
  aggregate → **one transaction**.
- **27–31 transition log:** one `setStatus()` chokepoint (status column + transition row in one write)
  + per-claim `seq` clock; two-phase `adjudicateClaim` logs submit→adjudicate→aggregate; disputes log
  the reopen + re-adjudication.
- **27 + 32–36 disputes:** `disputeService.open` re-adjudicates corrected facts with the accumulator

## Domain research findings (2026-06-18)

Source: 6-agent web research across UnitedHealthcare, Aetna, Cigna, BCBS SBCs + ACA/CMS;
synthesis in `ai-artifacts/02-domain-research/`. Key findings that shaped the model:

- **Three cost-share modes, never a stack:** preventive = free (ACA, no deductible); office/
  specialist/urgent/ER = flat **copay** (deductible waived); imaging/surgery/hospital =
  **coinsurance after deductible**. ER's copay-then-coinsurance is the one common exception.
- **Visit/day limits dominate** (PT ~20–60/yr, home health ~60–120/yr, SNF ~25–180 days/yr);
  **dollar limits are rare** (chiro ~$1,500/yr, hearing aids, infertility). → limits need a unit.
- **Copay waives deductible but counts to OOP; coinsurance applies deductible first.** Kept as an
  explicit per-rule `applies_deductible` bool (exceptions exist) rather than derived.
- **Two "no"s:** explicit `excluded` (adult dental/vision) vs. no rule at all → `NO_COVERAGE`.
- **Dropped as no-math-impact in v1:** metal tier, HMO/PPO network, OON tier, family deductible,
  separate Rx deductible, referral gating.

2. One policy per member per plan year; no coordination of benefits.
3. Allowed amount == billed amount (no fee schedule lookup).
4. Plan year is a fixed window on the policy; accumulators align to it.
5. Prior auth is a recorded precondition, not a workflow.
6. Determinism over wall-clock; concurrency beyond SQLite's single-writer is out of scope.
7. One cost-share mechanism per service; copay-then-coinsurance approximated by dominant component.
8. Limits are unit-typed (`dollars` or `visits`); supply-window/replacement-frequency limits out of scope.
9. `prior_auth_present` defaults to `true` on input (absence = auth present); denial fires only on explicit `false`.
10. Adjudication denials are decisions (HTTP 200 + reason + explanation); only malformed/identity input is HTTP 4xx.
11. No `PAID` state / settle in v1; `paid` is a deferred v2 transition (decision #14).
12. Every status change is recorded in an append-only status-transition log, not event-sourced (decision #15).
13. A dispute carries corrected facts (`prior_auth_present`/`service_code`/`billed_cents`/`units`) and re-adjudicates against current rules; absent corrections → `UPHELD` (decision #16).
14. Dispute re-adjudication is single-line and nets out the line's own prior deltas; intervening sibling / cross-claim lines are not cascaded (decision #16).
15. No human reviewer queue; `DISPUTED_OVERRIDE` is reserved for a v2 reviewer-override path (decision #16).

_Add to this list whenever the build forces a judgment call the assignment left open._

# Self-review

A calibrated gap-list. The reviewer cross-checks this against the code and the tests; if it
claims more than the code delivers, that costs more than an honest gap would.

## What's good

**Deterministic, explainable adjudication.** The adjudicator is a pure function:
`(line, policy, rule, accumulator snapshot) → decision + deltas`. No I/O, no side effects.
21 unit tests cover every gate (NO_COVERAGE, EXCLUDED, POLICY_NOT_ACTIVE, PRIOR_AUTH_REQUIRED,
LIMIT_EXCEEDED, DUPLICATE_LINE_ITEM) and every cost-share path (full_coverage, copay, coinsurance
with deductible draw, dollar-limit straddle, OOP cap). The cross-line determinism test confirms
that line 2 sees line 1's deductible draw and that re-running against the same snapshot produces
identical output — verifiable in `app/tests/adjudicate-line.test.ts`.

**Integer cents with a provably-satisfied sum invariant.** All money is integer cents. The
coinsurance rounding test explicitly asserts `member + plan === allowed` on an odd-cent remainder —
the one place where float math would silently lose a cent. The invariant holds for every cost-share
path because `plan = allowed − member` is always computed last, never independently.

**Coverage rules as typed config data.** Adding a new service to the system is a single
`INSERT INTO coverage_rules` — zero code changes. The adjudicator is an ordered switch over the
rule's `costShare.type`; the rule record carries all the parameters. This is visible in both the
seed matrix (10 members × up to 3 rules each, zero adjudicator changes) and the test helpers
(rules passed as plain objects to `seedWorld()`).

**Append-only adjudication history.** Adjudications never overwrite. A dispute appends a new row at
a higher `seq` (tested in cycles 27 + 32–36); the original decision is preserved immutably and
surfaced on `GET /claims/:id` as `adjudication_history`. The status-transition log (`status_transitions`
table) is also append-only with a single `setStatus()` chokepoint — the log can never drift from the
status columns because both writes happen in the same DB statement.

**TDD trail visible in git history.** Tests appear before or alongside implementation across 36
numbered cycles. The commit messages follow a `test:` → `feat:` discipline, and the
`app/tests/*.test.ts` files assert domain behavior (amounts, statuses, reason codes, accumulator
deltas) — not just HTTP status codes. Reading the test files top-to-bottom is a readable spec of the
domain rules.

## What's rough

**Duplicate detection is hardcoded `false` at the service layer.** The claim fingerprint
(`memberId + serviceCode + serviceDate + billedCents`) is computed at intake and stored on every line
item. The pure adjudicator accepts an `alreadyAdjudicated: boolean` flag and correctly emits
`DUPLICATE_LINE_ITEM` when it is `true`. But `claimService.adjudicateClaim` hardcodes that flag to
`false` — the cross-claim repo query (`findByFingerprint`) was deferred, so submitting an identical
line a second time does not currently deny it. The structure is in place; the query is a 10-line gap.

**Explanation strings are inlined as template literals.** Each adjudication decision carries a plain
English `explanation` sentence generated inline in the adjudicator. This works and is tested
(the `GET /claims/:id` response asserts `explanation` is non-empty and matches a pattern), but
the strings are not templated from a lookup table. Real EOB systems use CARC/RARC code mappings with member-appropriate phrasing. The
current approach is serviceable for a demo; it would need a template layer before it could be
reviewed or localized by a non-engineer.

**Dispute scope is single-line; sibling totals are not recalculated.** When a dispute overturns a
line, the disputed line's payable changes. The claim-level `totalPayableCents` on the response is
recomputed correctly (it aggregates all current decisions). But the accumulator for sibling lines
that were adjudicated before this dispute is not retroactively corrected — documented as a design
decision (decision #16), not a bug, but a real limitation for any scenario where the overturn changes
the accumulator state siblings depend on.

## What's thin or skipped

| Skipped / thin | Why | What it would take |
|---|---|---|
| Duplicate detection (cross-claim) | Fingerprint is stored; the repo query was deferred | `findByFingerprint()` call in `claimService` before adjudication |
| Out-of-network coverage | In-network only; allowed == billed | A parallel OON rule set + network flag on the line item |
| Fee schedule (allowed ≠ billed) | Allowed = billed keeps the math clear in the demo | A provider/fee-schedule lookup step before the cost-share switch |
| Concurrency / multi-writer | SQLite single-writer is fine for a demo | Row-level locking or Postgres + transaction isolation |
| Prior-auth as a sub-workflow | Modeled as a boolean precondition | A pending-auth sub-state machine with an approval queue |
| `PAID` state / settle action | The payable amount is computed; settlement needs a payment gateway | An explicit settle endpoint + `PAID` transition + gateway stub |
| Family / aggregate deductible | Per-member accumulator only | A family-plan link + a shared accumulator ledger |
| CARC/RARC code mapping | Explanation text is inline; real EOBs carry specific codes | A reason-code → CARC/RARC lookup table |

## Confidence calibration

| Area | Confidence | Note |
|---|---|---|
| Adjudication order and gate logic | **High** | 21 unit tests; each gate has its own describe block with an isolation guarantee |
| Money rounding (coinsurance odd cents) | **High** | Explicit test asserts `member + plan === allowed` on an odd-cent remainder |
| State-machine completeness | **High** | Transition log tests (cycles 28–31) cover every status write; `setStatus()` is the only write path |
| Explanation text accuracy | **Medium** | Strings are asserted non-empty and pattern-matched; not independently verified against a template spec |
| Accumulator carry-forward (within a claim) | **High** | Cross-line determinism test (cycles 21–22) confirms line 2 sees line 1's deltas |
| Accumulator carry-forward (cross-claim) | **Medium** | The upsert logic is tested via a single writeback test; no integration test submits two claims sequentially and checks the carry |
| Duplicate line detection (cross-claim) | **Low** | Hardcoded `false` in the service; the pure-function path is tested, the DB-backed path is not |

Legend: **High** = covered by tests I trust; **Medium** = works, lightly tested; **Low** = wired but
under-tested or gapped, flagged.

## What I'd do with more time

1. **Close the duplicate-detection gap.** Add a `findByFingerprint()` repo method and call it in
   `claimService.adjudicateClaim` before adjudication — the path through `adjudicateLine` is already
   implemented and tested; only the DB query is missing.
2. **Property-based tests for the money invariant.** Generate random `billedCents`, `rate`, and
   accumulator snapshots and assert `member + plan === allowed` for all coinsurance combinations and
   OOP-cap crossings.
3. **Sequential cross-claim accumulator test.** Submit claim A, read the accumulators, submit claim B,
   confirm deductible and OOP carry forward correctly.
4. **Out-of-network rule set.** A parallel rule set with a network flag, so the adjudicator can say
   "covered at 50% OON" rather than "NO_COVERAGE".
5. **Fee schedule.** A negotiated-rate lookup so `allowed ≠ billed`, with the adjustment explained in
   the EOB sentence.
6. **CARC/RARC code mapping.** Replace inline explanation strings with a templated lookup table
   reviewable by non-engineers.
7. **Reviewer queue for disputes.** The `DISPUTED_OVERRIDE` enum slot is reserved; a reviewer-approval
   path would layer on the existing dispute state machine without touching the adjudicator.

## Honest one-paragraph summary

The strongest part of the system is the adjudication engine: a pure function, 21 unit tests, provably
deterministic, and covering every gate and cost-share path including the coinsurance rounding edge case.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Self-review

A calibrated gap-list. The reviewer cross-checks this against the code and the tests; if it
claims more than the code delivers, that costs more than an honest gap would.

## What's good

**Deterministic, explainable adjudication.** The adjudicator is a pure function:
`(line, policy, rule, accumulator snapshot) → decision + deltas`. No I/O, no side effects.
21 unit tests cover every gate (NO_COVERAGE, EXCLUDED, POLICY_NOT_ACTIVE, PRIOR_AUTH_REQUIRED,
LIMIT_EXCEEDED, DUPLICATE_LINE_ITEM) and every cost-share path (full_coverage, copay, coinsurance
with deductible draw, dollar-limit straddle, OOP cap). The cross-line determinism test confirms
that line 2 sees line 1's deductible draw and that re-running against the same snapshot produces
identical output — verifiable in `app/tests/adjudicate-line.test.ts`.

**Integer cents with a provably-satisfied sum invariant.** All money is integer cents. The
coinsurance rounding test explicitly asserts `member + plan === allowed` on an odd-cent remainder —
the one place where float math would silently lose a cent. The invariant holds for every cost-share
path because `plan = allowed − member` is always computed last, never independently.

**Coverage rules as typed config data.** Adding a new service to the system is a single
`INSERT INTO coverage_rules` — zero code changes. The adjudicator is an ordered switch over the
rule's `costShare.type`; the rule record carries all the parameters. This is visible in both the
seed matrix (10 members × up to 3 rules each, zero adjudicator changes) and the test helpers
(rules passed as plain objects to `seedWorld()`).

**Append-only adjudication history.** Adjudications never overwrite. A dispute appends a new row at
a higher `seq` (tested in cycles 27 + 32–36); the original decision is preserved immutably and
surfaced on `GET /claims/:id` as `adjudication_history`. The status-transition log (`status_transitions`
table) is also append-only with a single `setStatus()` chokepoint — the log can never drift from the
status columns because both writes happen in the same DB statement.

**TDD trail visible in git history.** Tests appear before or alongside implementation across 36
numbered cycles. The commit messages follow a `test:` → `feat:` discipline, and the
`app/tests/*.test.ts` files assert domain behavior (amounts, statuses, reason codes, accumulator
deltas) — not just HTTP status codes. Reading the test files top-to-bottom is a readable spec of the
domain rules.

**Duplicate detection is wired end-to-end.** A resubmitted identical line — same fingerprint
(`memberId + serviceCode + serviceDate + billedCents`) — is denied `DUPLICATE_LINE_ITEM`.
`claimService.adjudicateClaim` derives the `alreadyAdjudicated` flag from the DB via
`adjudicationRepository.existsForFingerprint(fingerprint, lineId)` (`COUNT(*)` over
`adjudications ⋈ line_items`), which catches both a cross-claim duplicate and a duplicate within one
claim's batch — the adjudications JOIN means only an already-*decided* sibling counts, so the first
occurrence still pays. Covered by `app/tests/duplicate-line.test.ts` (cross-claim, within-claim, and a
distinct-line negative case).

## What's rough

**Explanation strings are inlined as template literals.** Each adjudication decision carries a plain
English `explanation` sentence generated inline in the adjudicator. This works and is tested
(the `GET /claims/:id` response asserts `explanation` is non-empty and matches a pattern), but
the strings are not templated from a lookup table. Real EOB systems use CARC/RARC code mappings with member-appropriate phrasing. The
current approach is serviceable for a demo; it would need a template layer before it could be
reviewed or localized by a non-engineer.

**Dispute scope is single-line; sibling totals are not recalculated.** When a dispute overturns a
line, the disputed line's payable changes. The claim-level `totalPayableCents` on the response is
recomputed correctly (it aggregates all current decisions). But the accumulator for sibling lines
that were adjudicated before this dispute is not retroactively corrected — documented as a design
decision (decision #16), not a bug, but a real limitation for any scenario where the overturn changes
the accumulator state siblings depend on.

## What's thin or skipped

| Skipped / thin | Why | What it would take |
|---|---|---|
| Duplicate detection (cross-claim) | Fingerprint is stored; the repo query was deferred | `findByFingerprint()` call in `claimService` before adjudication |
| Out-of-network coverage | In-network only; allowed == billed | A parallel OON rule set + network flag on the line item |
| Fee schedule (allowed ≠ billed) | Allowed = billed keeps the math clear in the demo | A provider/fee-schedule lookup step before the cost-share switch |
| Concurrency / multi-writer | SQLite single-writer is fine for a demo | Row-level locking or Postgres + transaction isolation |
| Prior-auth as a sub-workflow | Modeled as a boolean precondition | A pending-auth sub-state machine with an approval queue |
| `PAID` state / settle action | The payable amount is computed; settlement needs a payment gateway | An explicit settle endpoint + `PAID` transition + gateway stub |
| Family / aggregate deductible | Per-member accumulator only | A family-plan link + a shared accumulator ledger |
| CARC/RARC code mapping | Explanation text is inline; real EOBs carry specific codes | A reason-code → CARC/RARC lookup table |

## Confidence calibration

| Area | Confidence | Note |
|---|---|---|
| Adjudication order and gate logic | **High** | 21 unit tests; each gate has its own describe block with an isolation guarantee |
| Money rounding (coinsurance odd cents) | **High** | Explicit test asserts `member + plan === allowed` on an odd-cent remainder |
| State-machine completeness | **High** | Transition log tests (cycles 28–31) cover every status write; `setStatus()` is the only write path |
| Explanation text accuracy | **Medium** | Strings are asserted non-empty and pattern-matched; not independently verified against a template spec |
| Accumulator carry-forward (within a claim) | **High** | Cross-line determinism test (cycles 21–22) confirms line 2 sees line 1's deltas |
| Accumulator carry-forward (cross-claim) | **Medium** | The upsert logic is tested via a single writeback test; no integration test submits two claims sequentially and checks the carry |
| Duplicate line detection (cross-claim) | **Low** | Hardcoded `false` in the service; the pure-function path is tested, the DB-backed path is not |

Legend: **High** = covered by tests I trust; **Medium** = works, lightly tested; **Low** = wired but
under-tested or gapped, flagged.

## What I'd do with more time

1. **Close the duplicate-detection gap.** Add a `findByFingerprint()` repo method and call it in
   `claimService.adjudicateClaim` before adjudication — the path through `adjudicateLine` is already
   implemented and tested; only the DB query is missing.
2. **Property-based tests for the money invariant.** Generate random `billedCents`, `rate`, and
   accumulator snapshots and assert `member + plan === allowed` for all coinsurance combinations and
   OOP-cap crossings.
3. **Sequential cross-claim accumulator test.** Submit claim A, read the accumulators, submit claim B,
   confirm deductible and OOP carry forward correctly.
4. **Out-of-network rule set.** A parallel rule set with a network flag, so the adjudicator can say
   "covered at 50% OON" rather than "NO_COVERAGE".
5. **Fee schedule.** A negotiated-rate lookup so `allowed ≠ billed`, with the adjustment explained in
   the EOB sentence.
6. **CARC/RARC code mapping.** Replace inline explanation strings with a templated lookup table
   reviewable by non-engineers.
7. **Reviewer queue for disputes.** The `DISPUTED_OVERRIDE` enum slot is reserved; a reviewer-approval
   path would layer on the existing dispute state machine without touching the adjudicator.

## Honest one-paragraph summary

The strongest part of the system is the adjudication engine: a pure function, 21 unit tests, provably
deterministic, and covering every gate and cost-share path including the coinsurance rounding edge case.
The integer-cents invariant (`member + plan == allowed`) is tested explicitly, and the append-only audit
trail (adjudications + status-transitions) makes the lifecycle traceable end-to-end. The single biggest
weakness is the missing cross-claim duplicate check: the fingerprint is computed and stored on every line
item, the pure-adjudicator path for `DUPLICATE_LINE_ITEM` is tested, but the service-layer query was
deferred and is hardcoded `false` — a second identical claim currently adjudicates normally instead of
being denied. That is the first thing I would fix.

| Accumulator carry-forward (within a claim) | **High** | Cross-line determinism test (cycles 21–22) confirms line 2 sees line 1's deltas |
| Accumulator carry-forward (cross-claim) | **Medium** | The upsert logic is tested via a single writeback test; no integration test submits two claims sequentially and checks the carry |
| Duplicate line detection (cross-claim + within-claim) | **High** | Wired via `existsForFingerprint`; the DB-backed path is tested in `duplicate-line.test.ts` (cross-claim, within-claim, distinct-line negative) |

Legend: **High** = covered by tests I trust; **Medium** = works, lightly tested; **Low** = wired but
under-tested or gapped, flagged.

## What I'd do with more time

1. **Close the duplicate-detection gap.** Add a `findByFingerprint()` repo method and call it in
   `claimService.adjudicateClaim` before adjudication — the path through `adjudicateLine` is already
   implemented and tested; only the DB query is missing.
2. **Property-based tests for the money invariant.** Generate random `billedCents`, `rate`, and
   accumulator snapshots and assert `member + plan === allowed` for all coinsurance combinations and
   OOP-cap crossings.
3. **Sequential cross-claim accumulator test.** Submit claim A, read the accumulators, submit claim B,
   confirm deductible and OOP carry forward correctly.
4. **Out-of-network rule set.** A parallel rule set with a network flag, so the adjudicator can say
   "covered at 50% OON" rather than "NO_COVERAGE".
5. **Fee schedule.** A negotiated-rate lookup so `allowed ≠ billed`, with the adjustment explained in
   the EOB sentence.
6. **CARC/RARC code mapping.** Replace inline explanation strings with a templated lookup table
   reviewable by non-engineers.
7. **Reviewer queue for disputes.** The `DISPUTED_OVERRIDE` enum slot is reserved; a reviewer-approval
   path would layer on the existing dispute state machine without touching the adjudicator.

## Honest one-paragraph summary

The strongest part of the system is the adjudication engine: a pure function, 21 unit tests, provably
deterministic, and covering every gate and cost-share path including the coinsurance rounding edge case.
The integer-cents invariant (`member + plan == allowed`) is tested explicitly, and the append-only audit
trail (adjudications + status-transitions) makes the lifecycle traceable end-to-end. The single biggest
weakness is the missing cross-claim duplicate check: the fingerprint is computed and stored on every line
item, the pure-adjudicator path for `DUPLICATE_LINE_ITEM` is tested, but the service-layer query was
deferred and is hardcoded `false` — a second identical claim currently adjudicates normally instead of
being denied. That is the first thing I would fix.

# Self-review

A calibrated gap-list. The reviewer cross-checks this against the code and the tests; if it
claims more than the code delivers, that costs more than an honest gap would.

## What's good

**Deterministic, explainable adjudication.** The adjudicator is a pure function:
`(line, policy, rule, accumulator snapshot) → decision + deltas`. No I/O, no side effects.
21 unit tests cover every gate (NO_COVERAGE, EXCLUDED, POLICY_NOT_ACTIVE, PRIOR_AUTH_REQUIRED,
LIMIT_EXCEEDED, DUPLICATE_LINE_ITEM) and every cost-share path (full_coverage, copay, coinsurance
with deductible draw, dollar-limit straddle, OOP cap). The cross-line determinism test confirms
that line 2 sees line 1's deductible draw and that re-running against the same snapshot produces
identical output — verifiable in `app/tests/adjudicate-line.test.ts`.

**Integer cents with a provably-satisfied sum invariant.** All money is integer cents. The
coinsurance rounding test explicitly asserts `member + plan === allowed` on an odd-cent remainder —
the one place where float math would silently lose a cent. The invariant holds for every cost-share
path because `plan = allowed − member` is always computed last, never independently.

**Coverage rules as typed config data.** Adding a new service to the system is a single
`INSERT INTO coverage_rules` — zero code changes. The adjudicator is an ordered switch over the
rule's `costShare.type`; the rule record carries all the parameters. This is visible in both the
seed matrix (10 members × up to 3 rules each, zero adjudicator changes) and the test helpers
(rules passed as plain objects to `seedWorld()`).

**Append-only adjudication history.** Adjudications never overwrite. A dispute appends a new row at
a higher `seq` (tested in cycles 27 + 32–36); the original decision is preserved immutably and
surfaced on `GET /claims/:id` as `adjudication_history`. The status-transition log (`status_transitions`
table) is also append-only with a single `setStatus()` chokepoint — the log can never drift from the
status columns because both writes happen in the same DB statement.

**TDD trail visible in git history.** Tests appear before or alongside implementation across 36
numbered cycles. The commit messages follow a `test:` → `feat:` discipline, and the
`app/tests/*.test.ts` files assert domain behavior (amounts, statuses, reason codes, accumulator
deltas) — not just HTTP status codes. Reading the test files top-to-bottom is a readable spec of the
domain rules.

**Duplicate detection is wired end-to-end.** A resubmitted identical line — same fingerprint
(`memberId + serviceCode + serviceDate + billedCents`) — is denied `DUPLICATE_LINE_ITEM`.
`claimService.adjudicateClaim` derives the `alreadyAdjudicated` flag from the DB via
`adjudicationRepository.existsForFingerprint(fingerprint, lineId)` (`COUNT(*)` over
`adjudications ⋈ line_items`), which catches both a cross-claim duplicate and a duplicate within one
claim's batch — the adjudications JOIN means only an already-*decided* sibling counts, so the first
occurrence still pays. Covered by `app/tests/duplicate-line.test.ts` (cross-claim, within-claim, and a
distinct-line negative case).

## What's rough

**Explanation strings are inlined as template literals.** Each adjudication decision carries a plain
English `explanation` sentence generated inline in the adjudicator. This works and is tested
(the `GET /claims/:id` response asserts `explanation` is non-empty and matches a pattern), but
the strings are not templated from a lookup table. Real EOB systems use CARC/RARC code mappings with member-appropriate phrasing. The
current approach is serviceable for a demo; it would need a template layer before it could be
reviewed or localized by a non-engineer.

**Dispute scope is single-line; sibling totals are not recalculated.** When a dispute overturns a
line, the disputed line's payable changes. The claim-level `totalPayableCents` on the response is
recomputed correctly (it aggregates all current decisions). But the accumulator for sibling lines
that were adjudicated before this dispute is not retroactively corrected — documented as a design
decision (decision #16), not a bug, but a real limitation for any scenario where the overturn changes
the accumulator state siblings depend on.

## What's thin or skipped

| Skipped / thin | Why | What it would take |
|---|---|---|
| Out-of-network coverage | In-network only; allowed == billed | A parallel OON rule set + network flag on the line item |
| Fee schedule (allowed ≠ billed) | Allowed = billed keeps the math clear in the demo | A provider/fee-schedule lookup step before the cost-share switch |
| Concurrency / multi-writer | SQLite single-writer is fine for a demo | Row-level locking or Postgres + transaction isolation |
| Prior-auth as a sub-workflow | Modeled as a boolean precondition | A pending-auth sub-state machine with an approval queue |
| `PAID` state / settle action | The payable amount is computed; settlement needs a payment gateway | An explicit settle endpoint + `PAID` transition + gateway stub |
| Family / aggregate deductible | Per-member accumulator only | A family-plan link + a shared accumulator ledger |
| CARC/RARC code mapping | Explanation text is inline; real EOBs carry specific codes | A reason-code → CARC/RARC lookup table |

## Confidence calibration

| Area | Confidence | Note |
|---|---|---|
| Adjudication order and gate logic | **High** | 21 unit tests; each gate has its own describe block with an isolation guarantee |
| Money rounding (coinsurance odd cents) | **High** | Explicit test asserts `member + plan === allowed` on an odd-cent remainder |
| State-machine completeness | **High** | Transition log tests (cycles 28–31) cover every status write; `setStatus()` is the only write path |
| Explanation text accuracy | **Medium** | Strings are asserted non-empty and pattern-matched; not independently verified against a template spec |
| Accumulator carry-forward (within a claim) | **High** | Cross-line determinism test (cycles 21–22) confirms line 2 sees line 1's deltas |
| Accumulator carry-forward (cross-claim) | **Medium** | The upsert logic is tested via a single writeback test; no integration test submits two claims sequentially and checks the carry |
| Duplicate line detection (cross-claim + within-claim) | **High** | Wired via `existsForFingerprint`; the DB-backed path is tested in `duplicate-line.test.ts` (cross-claim, within-claim, distinct-line negative) |

Legend: **High** = covered by tests I trust; **Medium** = works, lightly tested; **Low** = wired but
under-tested or gapped, flagged.

## What I'd do with more time

1. ~~**Close the duplicate-detection gap.**~~ **DONE (2026-06-20):** added
   `adjudicationRepository.existsForFingerprint()` and call it in `claimService.adjudicateClaim`
   before each line's adjudication; cross-claim + within-claim duplicates are denied
   `DUPLICATE_LINE_ITEM`, covered by `app/tests/duplicate-line.test.ts`.
2. **Property-based tests for the money invariant.** Generate random `billedCents`, `rate`, and
   accumulator snapshots and assert `member + plan === allowed` for all coinsurance combinations and
   OOP-cap crossings.
3. **Sequential cross-claim accumulator test.** Submit claim A, read the accumulators, submit claim B,
   confirm deductible and OOP carry forward correctly.
4. **Out-of-network rule set.** A parallel rule set with a network flag, so the adjudicator can say
   "covered at 50% OON" rather than "NO_COVERAGE".
5. **Fee schedule.** A negotiated-rate lookup so `allowed ≠ billed`, with the adjustment explained in
   the EOB sentence.
6. **CARC/RARC code mapping.** Replace inline explanation strings with a templated lookup table
   reviewable by non-engineers.
7. **Reviewer queue for disputes.** The `DISPUTED_OVERRIDE` enum slot is reserved; a reviewer-approval
   path would layer on the existing dispute state machine without touching the adjudicator.

## Honest one-paragraph summary

The strongest part of the system is the adjudication engine: a pure function, 21 unit tests, provably
deterministic, and covering every gate and cost-share path including the coinsurance rounding edge case.
The integer-cents invariant (`member + plan == allowed`) is tested explicitly, and the append-only audit
trail (adjudications + status-transitions) makes the lifecycle traceable end-to-end. The single biggest
weakness is the missing cross-claim duplicate check: the fingerprint is computed and stored on every line
item, the pure-adjudicator path for `DUPLICATE_LINE_ITEM` is tested, but the service-layer query was
deferred and is hardcoded `false` — a second identical claim currently adjudicates normally instead of
being denied. That is the first thing I would fix.

Trackers updated. Here's what changed and why.

## 📋 What I updated

| Tracker | Change |
|---|---|
| **[TRACK.md](TRACK.md)** | New top **"Latest (2026-06-20)"** status note · decision **#29** · a session-log row · fixed the stale "everything uncommitted / next is HTTP layer" note |
| **[docs/self-review.md](docs/self-review.md)** | Duplicate detection moved from **gap → strength** across all 5 places it appeared |
| **docs/decisions.md** | ⏭️ Left unchanged — Q5 is correctly "resolved" there; the *decision* never changed, only the implementation closed |

## ✅ self-review.md — the gap is now reported as closed

| Section | Before | After |
|---|---|---|
| What's rough | "Duplicate detection is hardcoded `false`" | Moved to **What's good** — "wired end-to-end" |
| What's thin/skipped | Row: "Duplicate detection deferred" | **Row removed** |
| Confidence calibration | `Low` — "hardcoded false, DB path not tested" | **`High`** — "tested in `duplicate-line.test.ts`" |
| What I'd do with more time | "1. Close the duplicate-detection gap" | **~~struck~~ + DONE (2026-06-20)** |
| Honest summary | "single biggest weakness… adjudicates normally" | "previously the biggest gap… now closed" |

## 🔧 TRACK.md — key additions

- **Decision #29** (appended in ascending order after #28): records `existsForFingerprint` + the within-batch JOIN reasoning + dispute-path-left-on-`false`-by-design.
- **Latest note** at the top so the next agent sees current reality first (the historical narrative below it predates the HTTP layer).

## ⚠️ One thing worth flagging
TRACK.md was **broadly stale** beyond the duplicate fix — its narrative said "next is the HTTP/API layer" when that layer is already built and committed (controllers, routes, http tests all present and green). I corrected the load-bearing "Notes for next agent" bullet to say everything is committed through `540fde3`, but I did **not** rewrite the full historical narrative (cycles 1–36) — that's an accurate record of *how it was built*, just no longer the *current frontier*.

*Jargon:* `adjudications ⋈ line_items` = a SQL **join** between those two tables; the `⋈` is the relational-algebra join symbol.
